names.japanese_name: パラオキシ安息香酸エチル
names.japanese_label_name: パラオキシ安息香酸エチル

natural_occurrence.description: A synthetic ester of p-hydroxybenzoic acid. Part of the paraben family of preservatives with broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity against bacteria, yeasts, and molds. More commonly used in cosmetics than food due to controversy over potential endocrine-disrupting properties.

manufacturing.description: Produced by esterification of p-hydroxybenzoic acid with ethanol in the presence of an acid catalyst (typically sulfuric acid or hydrochloric acid). Can also be synthesized through the Kolbe-Schmitt reaction followed by esterification.

other_uses.cosmetics: Widely used in cosmetics, lotions, shampoos, and personal care products (up to 0.4%)
other_uses.industrial: Used in industrial coatings and adhesives
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Used in pharmaceutical preparations, tablet coatings, and topical medications

eu.conditions: Group ADI of 0-10 mg/kg bw/day for methyl and ethyl parabens; use restricted to specific food categories

usa.notes: Approved for food use; more commonly found in cosmetics and pharmaceuticals

japan.japanese_regulation_name: パラオキシ安息香酸エチル
japan.notes: Part of paraben group with usage standards; limited food applications

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: 0–10 mg/kg bw/day (group ADI for methyl and ethyl parabens)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: 10 mg/kg bw/day (group ADI for methyl and ethyl parabens and their sodium salts)

regulatory_history.event: EFSA establishes group ADI of 10 mg/kg bw/day for methyl and ethyl parabens
EU bans propylparaben (E216) and isopropylparaben from food due to endocrine concerns; methyl and ethyl parabens remain approved
EU bans isopropylparaben from cosmetics and food
European Commission classifies parabens as Category 1 endocrine disrupters in database

commonly_found_in: Cosmetics and personal care products (primary use)

adi_context.source_note: Based on JECFA/EFSA group ADI for methyl and ethyl parabens. CONTROVERSY: Parabens can mimic estrogen and have anti-androgenic effects. EU classified all parabens as Category 1 endocrine disrupters. Propyl and longer-chain parabens banned in EU food (2006) due to stronger hormonal activity. Methyl and ethyl parabens remain approved but controversial. Declining use in food; primarily used in cosmetics where limited dermal absorption reduces systemic exposure.
